title: Writing - Part 2: Exercise 9
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 220-260 words.

tasks.title: Report
tasks.prompt: Your manager at work has asked you to write a report on the effectiveness of a new software system that the company has been using for the last six months. Mention the benefits, the problems, and whether more training is needed.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Report on the Implementation of 'ProjectFlow' Software

Introduction
The purpose of this report is to evaluate the performance of the 'ProjectFlow' management system since its introduction six months ago. It identifies the impact on workflow and recommends future actions.

Operational Benefits
The primary advantage of the new system is the centralisation of data. Previously, project files were scattered across various local drives, leading to version control issues. ProjectFlow has eliminated this problem, allowing all team members to access real-time updates. Furthermore, the integrated 'Gantt Chart' feature has significantly improved our ability to visualise deadlines, resulting in a 15% increase in on-time project completion.

Identified Problems
However, the transition has not been without difficulties. Several staff members have reported that the user interface is counter-intuitive, particularly when generating monthly reports. This has led to a reliance on a few 'power users', creating a bottleneck in productivity. Additionally, the mobile application frequently crashes when uploading large attachments, which is a major issue for our field technicians.

Training Requirements
It is evident that the initial two-day training session was insufficient. Most staff are only using about 40% of the software's capabilities. I recommend holding advanced workshops tailored to different departments to ensure we are maximising our investment.

Conclusion
ProjectFlow is a powerful tool that has already improved our efficiency. However, to resolve the current frustrations, we must address the technical bugs in the mobile app and provide more comprehensive training for the staff.

tasks.title: Email
tasks.prompt: You have been offered an exciting job in another city, but it would mean leaving your current role earlier than expected. Write an email to your current boss, explaining the situation and asking if it would be possible to leave your post early.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Dear Mrs. Gable,

I am writing to share some significant personal news and to discuss my future with the company. I have recently been offered a senior position at a firm in London, a role that aligns perfectly with my long-term career goals in international trade.

While I am thrilled about this opportunity, the new role is scheduled to commence on the 1st of next month. As you know, my contract requires a two-month notice period, which would normally take me through to the end of the following month. However, given that my current projects are reaching their conclusion, I would like to respectfully ask if it might be possible to negotiate an early release from my contract.

I am fully committed to ensuring a smooth transition. Over the next two weeks, I am prepared to work extra hours to complete all outstanding documentation and to assist in the handover of my responsibilities to my successor. I have already drafted a detailed manual for the upcoming 'Spring Campaign' to ensure the team is well-prepared.

I have truly valued my time here and am grateful for the opportunities you have provided me. I hope we can find a solution that supports my career progression while minimising any inconvenience to the department.

I would appreciate the chance to discuss this with you in person at your earliest convenience.

Best regards,
Thomas Wright

tasks.title: Review
tasks.prompt: You have seen this post on a student blog: 'We want reviews of online courses you have taken. Tell us about the course content, the quality of the teaching, and whether you think it's better than learning in a traditional classroom.' Write your review.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Mastering Digital Photography: An Online Perspective

Last month, I completed the 'Advanced Digital Photography' course on the EduVantage platform. As someone who has always preferred traditional classroom learning, I was pleasantly surprised by the quality of this digital experience.

The course content was exceptionally comprehensive. It covered everything from the technical nuances of manual settings to the artistic theories of composition and post-processing. What I found particularly useful were the 'weekly challenges', where we had to submit photos based on a specific theme. This ensured that the theoretical knowledge was immediately put into practice.

The quality of teaching was also a standout feature. The instructor, a professional National Geographic photographer, provided pre-recorded lectures that were both clear and visually stunning. Furthermore, the feedback provided on our assignments was personalised and detailed, which is something I had feared would be missing in an online format.

Is it better than a traditional classroom? In some ways, yes. The ability to pause and re-watch complex technical explanations is a major advantage. However, you do miss out on the spontaneous peer-to-peer discussions that happen in a physical studio. For a technical subject like photography, the flexibility of online learning is hard to beat, but for those who thrive on social interaction, it might feel a bit isolating.

Overall, I would highly recommend this course to any aspiring photographer. It is excellent value for money and provides a professional level of instruction from the comfort of your own home.
